translation: A supplicant asked for a punishment bound to happen
commentary:
سَأَلَ سَآئِلٌۢ بِعَذَابٍ وَاقِعٍ

Surah Al-Ma'arij opens with a striking and unusual format: "A questioner asks about punishment that is bound to drop, that is bound to occur." The word سَأَلَ could be replaced with other forms of questioning, but Allah ﷻ specifically chose this construction to convey something deeper about the nature of the questioner. The use of سَآئِلٌ suggests someone who loves to ask—not out of genuine curiosity, but from cynicism and mockery. This is the critical, condescending type of person who asks, "Where is this punishment you keep talking about? How is it going to come? What will it look like?" The tone is one of derision, challenging the very reality of divine accountability.

translation: To the disbelievers; of it there is no preventer
commentary:
لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ لَيْسَ لَهُۥ دَافِعٌ

Allah ﷻ responds directly to this mockery: "For the disbelievers—there will be no one to defend against it." Notice that He doesn't say "for him" but rather "for the disbelievers," because the questioner represents an entire group of people who share this attitude of denial. The word دَافِعٌ means to defend someone or to push an attack away in order to shield them. When that day comes, nothing and no one will be able to shield these disbelievers from what awaits them.

translation: [It is] from Allah, owner of the ways of ascent
commentary:
مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ذِى ٱلْمَعَارِجِ

This punishment comes "from Allah, the Possessor of great heights." The word الْمَعَارِجِ comes from عَرَجَ, meaning to climb stairs or ascend—going higher and higher. Allah ﷻ is the Lord of the seven heavens, the One who exists above all creation in majesty and authority.

translation: The angels and the Spirit will ascend to Him during a Day the extent of which is fifty thousand years
commentary:
تَعْرُجُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَٱلرُّوحُ إِلَيْهِ فِى يَوْمٍ كَانَ مِقْدَارُهُۥ خَمْسِينَ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ

The angels and the Spirit (Jibreel AS) ascend toward Him in a day whose measure is fifty thousand years. This describes both the duration and the intensity of Judgment Day. We must understand that our conception of "day" is tied to the rotation of the earth and the presence of the sun. On Judgment Day, the earth will be stretched out, and the sun and moon will be gone—so our familiar measure of time becomes meaningless. But if you want to grasp some idea of how long and how intense that process will be, imagine fifty thousand years.

translation: So be patient with gracious patience
commentary:
فَٱصْبِرْ صَبْرًا جَمِيلًا

After describing the magnitude of that Day, Allah ﷻ commands the Prophet ﷺ: "Then show the most beautiful form of patience." Even when people come to you in the most condescending tone, when they speak to you in insulting ways about your beliefs—show beautiful patience. This is something we struggle with today. When people speak insultingly about Islam, we often fire right back, matching their anger with our own. If they troll, we troll back. But the instruction here is clear: ignore the ignorant and do not respond in kind. Beautiful patience means rising above the provocation.

translation: Indeed, they see it [as] distant
commentary:
إِنَّهُمْ يَرَوْنَهُۥ بَعِيدًا

They see Judgment Day as something very far away. In their view, it's nowhere near coming.

translation: But We see it [as] near
commentary:
وَنَرَىٰهُ قَرِيبًا

However, We see it as near. This ayah presents a powerful lesson about perspective. Consider someone traveling on a road—how far ahead can they see? Their view is limited by the terrain, by trees on either side, by the curves in the road. Now consider someone traveling by air in the same direction—their view extends much farther. The one above can see what lies ahead and what lies behind; they have a view that is far more expansive.

Our view of Judgment Day is like that limited road traveler. We think we have a long way to go. But Allah's view of events is like that aerial perspective. He sees the entire scenery of time, the complete tapestry of history and future all at once. So as far as He is concerned, He sees it very close. It's entirely a matter of perspective—and we should never assume that distance means safety.

translation: On the Day the sky will be like murky oil
commentary:
يَوْمَ تَكُونُ ٱلسَّمَآءُ كَٱلْمُهْلِ

The Day when the sky will look like molten oil or molten metal. The word الْمُهْلِ refers to substances that have been melted and are pouring out, losing their solid form entirely.

translation: And the mountains will be like wool
commentary:
وَتَكُونُ ٱلْجِبَالُ كَٱلْعِهْنِ

The mountains will become like colored wool—flaky, soft, shredded into small pieces. This continues the conversation from the previous surah, where we learned how mountains will be pulverized and scattered as dust.

translation: And no friend will ask [anything of] a friend
commentary:
وَلَا يَسْـَٔلُ حَمِيمٌ حَمِيمًا

Not a single close friend will ask about his close friend. The word حَمِيمٌ comes from حَمّ, which means intense heat—like fever. It can also refer to intense, feverish love for someone. A hamim is your closest, most intimate friend—yet on that Day, no one will ask about even their dearest companion.

translation: They will be shown each other. The criminal will wish that he could be ransomed from the punishment of that Day by his children
commentary:
يُبَصَّرُونَهُمْ ۚ يَوَدُّ ٱلْمُجْرِمُ لَوْ يَفْتَدِى مِنْ عَذَابِ يَوْمِئِذٍۭ بِبَنِيهِ

Despite being shown to each other—they will be made to look at one another very thoroughly—they still won't care about the other. It's not a case of "out of sight, out of mind." They will literally see their closest friends and family, and yet still not inquire about them.

The criminal will wish that he could give ransom to escape the punishment of that Day—by offering his own children. يَفْتَدِى means to give up something in exchange, to pay a ransom. He will say, "Take my children into Hellfire and let me go! Don't hurt me."

This is the complete opposite of human nature in this world. If your child is about to face danger, you would take that danger upon yourself without hesitation. You would forget about defending yourself and defend your child at any cost. This instinct exists in Muslims and non-Muslims alike—it's even found in animals. When a predator tries to eat the eggs in a nest, the mother bird covers them with her wings, taking the attack herself. An animal that normally runs from a lion will turn back and fight when its young is being attacked.

But on Judgment Day, the terror is so overwhelming that even these most basic natural instincts are overridden by fear. Notice, however, that Allah ﷻ specifies "the criminal"—this description applies to the guilty, not to all human beings. In contrast, we learned that the people of Paradise will ask about their friends, showing concern even in the afterlife.

translation: And his wife and his brother
commentary:
وَصَـٰحِبَتِهِۦ وَأَخِيهِ

His wife and his brother. Offering to give up one's spouse might not seem as shocking, but even one's own sibling—someone you grew up with, shared your life with—will be offered as ransom.

translation: And his nearest kindred who shelter him
commentary:
وَفَصِيلَتِهِ ٱلَّتِى تُـْٔوِيهِ

His close family that sheltered him. فَصِيلَة refers to the immediate family unit—parents, children, the small core family that took care of him, that gave him refuge and support throughout his life.

translation: And whoever is on earth entirely [so] then it could save him
commentary:
وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ يُنجِيهِ

And whoever is on the earth altogether, if only it would save him. If offering his immediate family doesn't work, he would gladly offer the entire population of humanity—every single person on earth—just to rescue himself from what awaits.

translation: No! Indeed, it is the Flame [of Hell]
commentary:
كَلَّآ ۖ إِنَّهَا لَظَىٰ

No—never! It is لَظَىٰ, a Fire that has matured. When fire first ignites, it gives off sparks and embers that pop out. But when those sparks stop and the fire has been burning steadily for a long time, it becomes something far more intense and sustained. This is that kind of fire.

translation: A remover of exteriors
commentary:
نَزَّاعَةً لِّلشَّوَىٰ

Relentlessly pulling at the limbs. نَزَّاعَةً means it continues to pull and tug. الشَّوَىٰ refers to the side limbs of the body—the arms and legs—but it also means the skin. This Fire keeps pulling on the limbs, and it keeps peeling off the skin. It literally rips the skin away, then another layer of skin is placed, and the Fire rips it off again—a cycle of unimaginable agony.

translation: It invites he who turned his back [on truth] and went away [from obedience]
commentary:
تَدْعُوا۟ مَنْ أَدْبَرَ وَتَوَلَّىٰ

It calls the one who showed his back and turned away. The Fire itself will call to him, saying "Come here," inviting him in. When he tries to turn away and escape, the Fire pulls him back. This also refers to his life in this world—the one who showed his back to guidance, who turned away from the warnings he received.

translation: And collected [wealth] and hoarded
commentary:
وَجَمَعَ فَأَوْعَىٰٓ

He collected wealth and placed it inside containers. أَوْعَىٰ comes from وِعَاء, meaning to put something in a container or vessel. He saved and stored and hoarded—putting money in savings, in bank accounts, in storage—never once considering helping another human being. This continues where the previous surah left off: "He did not encourage the feeding of the poor." And what did he do with his wealth instead? He collected it and kept storing it away, accumulating more and more without ever spending it in the way of Allah ﷻ.

translation: Indeed, mankind was created anxious
commentary:
۞ إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ خُلِقَ هَلُوعًا

No doubt about it—the human being has been created هَلُوعًا. This word describes someone who has no patience and no control over his temptations. The opposite of patience (صَبْر) in Arabic is جَزَع, which means complaining and not being patient. But halu'a goes even further: it means that whenever a temptation appears before you, you immediately rush toward it without thinking twice. You are the ideal consumer, the marketer's dream customer. You see a product and you buy it. You're the shopaholic, the one that companies want on their website because they know you'll consume and consume and consume without consideration. This is how the human being was created—quick to consume without restraint, without pausing to think about consequences.

translation: When evil touches him, impatient
commentary:
إِذَا مَسَّهُ ٱلشَّرُّ جَزُوعًا

When harm touches him, he loses patience entirely. First, you're quick to consume. But when harm comes as a consequence—and if you're so quick to consume without consideration, there will inevitably be consequences—he becomes جَزُوعًا, completely impatient. "Why is this happening to me? I deserve better!"

A father approached me at a youth conference and mentioned a problem he's noticed in the community: teenage children constantly complaining that they don't have enough. "You didn't even let me get a new phone. How come I don't have a computer in my room?" Yet whenever you ask them to do something—take out the garbage, go to the masjid—they respond, "I have homework. I'm tired. I can't do it." Quick to consume, but also quick to complain. These two traits have come together in a generation: self-absorbed with zero consideration for others.

translation: And when good touches him, withholding [of it]
commentary:
وَإِذَا مَسَّهُ ٱلْخَيْرُ مَنُوعًا

And when good touches him, he becomes مَنُوعًا —extremely withholding and preventative. When something good comes your way, you think, "How do I make sure others don't know I have this? Because they might want to share in it, and I don't want to share." You want to hold onto it all for yourself.

The case study of this behavior already appeared in the Quran: remember the farmers who planned to harvest their crop early in the morning? They said to each other, "Don't let any poor people find you, because then we might have to give them something." This is the living example of manu'a—hoarding blessings and refusing to share them with those in need.

translation: Except the observers of prayer
commentary:
إِلَّا ٱلْمُصَلِّينَ

Except the people of prayer. Allah ﷻ is saying that these moral disorders—these personality flaws of greed, selfishness, impatience and complaint—are addressed by salah. The question naturally arises: why aren't they addressed by my prayer and yours? There must be something missing in our salah, because if it truly was salah as Allah ﷻ intended, then these character flaws would be corrected. Allah ﷻ Himself tells us elsewhere: "Indeed, prayer prevents shamelessness and all forms of evil deeds" (29:45). You hear this quoted in every khutbah, yet we still struggle with these very issues.

Prayer is meant to bring about transformation in two ways. I recently discussed the concept of تَدَبُّر —deep reflection—in Islam and the Quran. What does reflection truly mean? The word comes from the root د-ب-ر, similar to أَدْبَرَ which we saw earlier, meaning "turned back." When you merely glance at something, you've seen it. But when you do tadabbur of it, you see all sides of it—you even examine what's behind it. You've explored it thoroughly until you have a complete view.

Tadabbur in the Quran means you don't just read the ayah—you investigate deeply to discover what's behind it. What lesson is there for me? What sign is Allah ﷻ showing me? When the ayah says "the human being was created greedy, quick to consume," the tadabbur is asking: what is this saying about me as a person?

Tadabbur itself has two dimensions. On one hand, it's spiritual in nature—the Quran is a reminder for the heart, a means of remembrance. On the other hand, tadabbur is also an intellectual process—reflection and thought in pursuit of wisdom. How do we know it has both dimensions? Because Allah ﷻ complains twice in the Quran about people not doing tadabbur, addressing each aspect separately.

First, He says: "Do they not reflect upon the Quran, or are there locks upon their hearts?" (47:24). When He mentions locked hearts, He's addressing a spiritual problem. Reflection on the Quran isn't happening because of a spiritual blockage—and conversely, reflection on the Quran will solve that spiritual problem.

Second, He says: "Do they not reflect upon the Quran? If it had been from other than Allah, they would have found much contradiction in it" (4:82). Finding contradiction or consistency is an intellectual exercise. He's saying that if you truly reflected on the Quran, you would come to the intellectual conviction that there are no inconsistencies in it, no contradictions—it must be the word of Allah ﷻ.

So tadabbur has these two sides: spiritual and intellectual. But when do we engage with the Book in this way? When does tadabbur happen? It should happen in salah itself. Prayer is the exercise that follows engagement with the Book. When we recite Quran in prayer, that is meant to be the moment of reflection and thought.

Why is that the ideal time? Because genuine thinking requires that you're not distracted by other things. Allah ﷻ created this institution of salah in which you're forced to leave everything else behind. You cannot conduct business, check your phone, make calls or talk to anyone. You're not even allowed to look around or make unnecessary movements. You are physically and mentally restrained in your situation and in what you say. It's the perfect opportunity to truly leave the world behind and genuinely reflect on His words. This is how salah transforms us—when we pray with presence and contemplation, connecting deeply with the meanings we recite. This is what saves us from becoming the halu'a person described in these ayahs.

translation: Those who are constant in their prayer
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلَىٰ صَلَاتِهِمْ دَآئِمُونَ

Allah ﷻ now qualifies who these praying people are: *alladhīna hum 'alā ṣalātihim dā'imūn* (those who are constant especially when it comes to their prayers). The word "especially" captures something significant about the Arabic construction—*'alā* here emphasizes particular focus and care.

Notice that Allah ﷻ uses the singular *ṣalātihim* (their prayer) rather than *ṣalawātihim* (their prayers). The singular form in Arabic grammar often refers to the essence of something, the fundamental core. This isn't just about doing all the prayers—it's about the foundational, essential prayers that form the bedrock of their discipline. They are *dā'imūn* (constant, consistent, disciplined) regarding these essential prayers. They don't delay, procrastinate or treat these prayers casually.

This consistency establishes a minimum requirement, a baseline discipline in the life of a believer. Without this discipline, there's room for everything else to slip. But when this foundation is solid, it creates the framework for all the other qualities Allah ﷻ is about to describe.

## Ayah 24-25

وَٱلَّذِينَ فِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ حَقٌّ مَّعْلُومٌ • لِّلسَّآئِلِ وَٱلْمَحْرُومِ

The transition from prayer to wealth is immediate and deliberate: *wa alladhīna fī amwālihim ḥaqqun ma'lūm* (and those who in their assets and their money there is a known right). We go straight from prayer to giving, but this isn't simply a fundraising strategy. Allah ﷻ began this passage with greed as humanity's fundamental problem. He then prescribed prayer as the remedy. Now He shows us the result: once you truly engage in conscious prayer, you'll understand your real purpose in life, and you won't be as clingy with your money anymore.

When prayer involves genuine personal reflection, you realize that you're going to leave this world. You start making the right investments—eternal ones. No matter what passage of the Quran you recite in prayer, it will remind you that you're temporary here, that there are bigger concerns ahead. This realization naturally makes you think about the rights of others.

The right in your wealth belongs to two categories of people: *lis-sā'il wal-maḥrūm* (for the one who asks and the one who is deprived). The *sā'il* is someone who physically comes and, despite the humiliation, asks for help. Perhaps they lost a job, can't pay bills, the electricity was cut off, a spouse passed away or left, and they find themselves unable to manage. It hurts human dignity to have to ask. Being put in that position is embarrassing and humiliating. May Allah ﷻ protect us all from having to be in such a position. But when someone does bring themselves to ask, don't make their asking harder. Don't add to their embarrassment.

Then there's *al-maḥrūm*—the one who is deprived but doesn't ask. This category raises a profound question: How will you know about the *sā'il*, and especially, how will you know about the *maḥrūm*? The *maḥrūm* doesn't ask, yet you have a right in your wealth for them. This means you'll have to go ask them. You'll have to be in touch with people around you, aware of what's happening in your neighborhood, among your cousins, within your extended family.

We sometimes have an artificial concept of community where people pray together at the masjid, say salam to each other and leave. We don't truly know each other's families or problems. We're not close enough that someone would feel comfortable saying, "Brother, I have an issue in my family. I need help." When someone does come asking, we don't even know if they're genuine because we lack genuine relationships.

Behind this ayah is actually a call to build a sense of community and care for extended family. It starts with family, then expands to everyone else—relatives in need whether nearby or in another country, whether Muslim or not. Anyone who asks, anyone who is deprived—this is whom we're obligated to help.

translation: And those within whose wealth is a known right
For the petitioner and the deprived
And those who believe in the Day of Recompense
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُصَدِّقُونَ بِيَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ

*Wa alladhīna yuṣaddiqūna bi-yawm id-dīn* (and those who confirm the truth in the Day of ultimate judgment). These two acts—prayer and charity—are themselves proof of belief in the Hereafter. Prayer proves it because you gave up time, and time is an asset, sometimes more valuable than money. Charity proves it because you're willing to part with wealth. You're willing to give up both major assets—time and money—because you know of much better time and much better wealth coming your way. That's confirmation that you truly believe in what's ahead.

## Ayah 27-28

وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم مِّنْ عَذَابِ رَبِّهِم مُّشْفِقُونَ • إِنَّ عَذَابَ رَبِّهِمْ غَيْرُ مَأْمُونٍ

*Wa alladhīna hum min 'adhābi rabbihim mushfiqūn* (and those who, especially when it comes to the punishment of their Master, from it they are terrified). The word *mushfiqūn* carries a deeper meaning than simple fear. *Ishfāq* actually includes care and concern for loved ones—it means "I'm afraid for my children." They're afraid of their Master's punishment not just for themselves, but for others as well. The word is grammatically transitive by nature, implying concern that extends beyond the self.

Allah ﷻ has instilled in us the importance of giving to others in this passage, which means caring about others. If you care about people having trouble in this world, why wouldn't you care about them in the Hereafter? If you're worried someone might starve here, why wouldn't it worry you that they might face punishment there? Your own salvation terrifies you, and their salvation should terrify you too.

*Inna 'adhāba rabbihim ghayru ma'mūn* (no doubt the punishment of their Master is not something that anybody can be safe from). This confirms the reality: no one should feel complacent about escaping Allah's punishment. The fear is well-founded and should drive both personal righteousness and concern for others.

## Ayah 29-31

وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حَـٰفِظُونَ • إِلَّا عَلَىٰٓ أَزْوَٰجِهِمْ أَوْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ غَيْرُ مَلُومِينَ • فَمَنِ ٱبْتَغَىٰ وَرَآءَ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْعَادُونَ

*Wa alladhīna hum li-furūjihim ḥāfiẓūn* (and those who especially when it comes to their privates, they guard them). Allah ﷻ now addresses shame and modesty—they are not shameless people. The *wāw* (and) at the beginning lets us know this could be separate from the previous qualities, suggesting there could be people who take care of their prayer and do good things but don't watch themselves in this area. But all of these qualities must go together. The repeated structure *alladhīna... alladhīna... alladhīna* (those who... those who... those who) indicates we're still describing *al-muṣallīn*—the people who truly pray.

This is Allah's comprehensive definition of people who pray. It's not just about showing up at the masjid. People who pray are constant in their prayer, they have a known right in their wealth for those who ask, they guard their modesty and shame.

*Illā 'alā azwājihim aw mā malakat aymānuhum fa-innahum ghayru malūmīn* (except for their own spouses or what their right hands possess, then they're not to be blameworthy). The grammatical form *ghayru malūmīn* (not blameworthy) comes from the word *lawm* (blame). They are not to be blamed—no blame can be put on them for lawful intimate relations.

Allah ﷻ mentions guarding one's modesty right after discussing prayer because, as we learned earlier in the surah, *innā ṣ-ṣalāta tanhā 'an il-faḥshā'* (prayer prevents shamelessness). If they're really *muṣallīn*, then obviously they guard their modesty. Obviously they'll be people of *ḥayā'* (modesty and shame).

*Fa-man ibtaghā warā'a dhālika fa-ulā'ika humu l-'ādūn* (but whoever goes beyond those limits and pursues something else, they're the ones who have engaged in animosity, who have done an act of aggression). This completes the boundary—inside these limits is purity, beyond them is transgression.

translation: And those who are fearful of the punishment of their Lord
Indeed, the punishment of their Lord is not that from which one is safe
And those who guard their private parts
Except from their wives or those their right hands possess, for indeed, they are not to be blamed
But whoever seeks beyond that, then they are the transgressors
And those who are to their trusts and promises attentive
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِأَمَـٰنَـٰتِهِمْ وَعَهْدِهِمْ رَٰعُونَ

*Wa alladhīna hum li-amānātihim wa 'ahdihim rā'ūn* (and those who especially when it comes to the trusts they are entrusted with and the promises that they make, they are particularly and constantly watchful). The word *rā'ūn* actually means shepherding. A shepherd watches every single one of his sheep. Even if a wolf threatens to take away one sheep, the shepherd doesn't think, "Well, I still have 23 others." He's constantly looking around for anything that might attack any of his flock, constantly steering them in the right direction.

Every commitment we make should be treated like this because we are people of prayer. Prayer itself is a fulfillment of promise to Allah ﷻ, an *amānah* (trust) with Allah ﷻ—a promise that we'll come stand before Him. When you become indoctrinated into that reality, you realize the value of all promises.

One of the summaries of our entire religion appears in the Quran: *Yā ayyuha alladhīna āmanū awfū bil-'uqūd* (O you who believe, fulfill binding agreements). Our entire faith can be summarized in this. We have binding agreements with Allah ﷻ, with His Messenger ﷺ, with His Book, with each other, with our parents, spouse, children, siblings, neighbors, business contracts—they're all binding agreements.

When you watch these agreements carefully, you become punctual. You become careful about what you promise and the commitments you make. You get better at saying no. Especially younger people need to learn this: when you're doing religious service, people appreciate what you're doing and keep asking, "Can you do this? Can you come here?" You feel bad and say yes to everything. Before you know it, you're burnt out and unable to fulfill commitments. Don't put yourself in that position. Learn to say no when necessary. And when you do say yes, stick to it.

This applies to all matters. The Arabs have a saying: reprimanding your friend is better than losing a friend. I've experienced this—when friends have agreements and don't fulfill their end, and you don't say anything, the friendship deteriorates until you lose it entirely. If you're going to have an agreement, fulfill it. If it's not being fulfilled, remind the other person gently: "This is an agreement. You have to do your part."

We have to hold ourselves to a different standard in business ethics, work ethics, corporate dealings and entrepreneurial ventures. Every engagement we make must operate at a higher level. We should be extraordinarily cautious about honesty and dishonesty in every situation. This isn't about who's watching or what we can get away with—it's about being answerable to Allah ﷻ. Don't do shady things. This is part of your agreement to be truthful and honest in all circumstances.

translation: And those who are in their testimonies upright
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم بِشَهَـٰدَٰتِهِمْ قَآئِمُونَ

*Wa alladhīna hum bi-shahādātihim qā'imūn* (and people who, when it comes to the testimony that they give, they stand upright). Notice Allah ﷻ didn't say they are truthful in their testimony—that's already assumed. Rather, He emphasized that they *stand up* and give testimony. In other words, when you witness something and you know justice will not be served until you bear witness, you speak out. You get over your fear and stand up because you learned through prayer to fear only Allah ﷻ and therefore not fear anybody else. When the time comes to testify, you stand and give testimony.

These are the consequences of prayer. If you're really making prayer with consciousness, this is how it begins changing your personality—you speak out. This doesn't just mean in court or witnessing a crime. Sometimes your own mother says something wrong and uses it to create a bigger fight in the family, and you witnessed it. You can't just say, "That's my mom, I can't say anything." Yes, you can. This doesn't mean being disrespectful, but the truth is the truth.

Sometimes we say "family first," but no—truth first. Ibrahim (AS) didn't say "family first." The companions of the Prophet ﷺ didn't say "family first." Abu Talib didn't embrace Islam despite his love for the Prophet ﷺ. Truth and honesty come first.

There's a famous story from the time of Umar (RA): he was passing by a house and overheard a mother telling her daughter to pour water into the milk to increase its volume. The daughter refused, saying the Amir al-Mu'minin had forbidden it. The mother responded that Umar (RA) wasn't there to see. The daughter understood something profound: even if Umar (RA) isn't watching, Allah ﷻ is. She disobeyed her mother's instruction to engage in deception—not out of disrespect, but out of commitment to truth.

Sometimes you have to remind elders. Sometimes elders have to remind the young. This is what *alladhīna hum bi-shahādātihim qā'imūn* means. It's difficult, but this is the life of a Muslim, and it all flows from conscious, transformative prayer.

translation: And those who [carefully] maintain their prayer
commentary:
**وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلَىٰ صَلَاتِهِمْ يُحَافِظُونَ**

"And those who guard their prayers vigilantly."

This ayah completes the portrait of the believers by returning to where it began—with prayer. The word يُحَافِظُونَ carries profound implications. It means not merely to observe prayer, but to guard it, to protect it, to create a protective perimeter around it. This protection operates on two essential levels.

The first level concerns the times of prayer. Guarding your prayer means respecting its boundaries with discipline and foresight. If you go to sleep at 2:00 AM and tell yourself you'll wake up for Fajr, you're not guarding that prayer. True guardianship means going to sleep early at night to protect Fajr. When Isha comes in, you pray it immediately. You don't tell yourself there are eight hours until Fajr and take "just a little nap," only to wake up five minutes before the time expires. Guarding Maghrib means watching for its entrance and praying without delay, not continuing your conversation while the blue fades from the sky, waiting until the last possible moment.

The second level of guardianship encompasses everything this passage has taught us about the characteristics of people of prayer. When you understand this surah as a complete teaching, guarding your prayer means guarding all these qualities—honesty, trustworthiness, fulfilling promises, protecting your gaze, guarding your private parts. You cannot claim to be guarding your prayer while remaining a cheater, while being untrustworthy, while breaking your promises. The prayer you guard is not merely the physical act; it's the entire ethical framework that prayer is meant to establish in your life.

These ayahs describe the people of الصَّلَاةm—people who truly establish prayer in their lives. This is distinct from simply being believers. In other passages of the Quran, Allah ﷻ describes the qualities of people of iman, but here the focus is specifically on those who maintain prayer with all that it entails. The earlier ayahs warned that the Fire peels the skin off of people and calls to them—except those who pray. Prayer itself becomes protection from the Fire. It shields you not merely by its ritual performance, but by how it transforms and protects your character.

translation: They will be in gardens, honored
commentary:
**أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ مُّكْرَمُونَ**

"Those are the ones who will be in gardens, honored."

The reward for these guardians of prayer is decisive and beautiful. They will be in جَنَّـٰتٍ —multiple gardens, not just one. And there they will be مُّكْرَمُونَ —honored, dignified, elevated in status. The word carries connotations of nobility and respect, a complete contrast to the humiliation that awaits those who rejected faith.

translation: So what is [the matter] with those who disbelieve, hastening [from] before you, [O Muhammad]
commentary:
**فَمَالِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ قِبَلَكَ مُهْطِعِينَ**

"So what is wrong with those who disbelieve, rushing toward you?"

The surah now shifts dramatically. After painting this portrait of the believers and their reward, Allah ﷻ addresses a scene that was unfolding in Makkah. فَمَالِ —"What is wrong with?"—expresses divine amazement at their behavior. The word مُهْطِعِينَ describes a specific kind of running: racing behind a leader obediently, rushing forward in groups.

The context is crucial. As the Messenger of Allah ﷺ would recite these ayahs—which at that time were not merely recitation but active preaching, living sermon—the disbelievers would be insulted. When they heard descriptions of honesty, trustworthiness, and moral restraint, they knew they represented the opposite. When they heard about who would escape the Fire, they felt accused. So they would gather in groups and rush toward the Prophet ﷺ to interrupt, to intimidate, to sabotage the message. "Are you saying we're going to burn in Hell? Are you dishonoring our ancestors? Who are you to judge us?"

This pattern hasn't disappeared. Even today, when the characteristics of the righteous are described, some people immediately become defensive: "Are you saying everyone else is burning in Hell? Are you saying there are no other good people?" The question itself reveals discomfort with the standard being presented.

translation: [To sit] on [your] right and [your] left in separate groups
commentary:
**عَنِ ٱلْيَمِينِ وَعَنِ ٱلشِّمَالِ عِزِينَ**

"From the right and from the left, in groups."

They would come عِزِينَ —in small groups, each with its own leader and followers, trying to create the appearance of greater numbers and more substantial opposition. It was a tactic of intimidation: surround the message from all sides, make it seem like everyone is against it, overwhelm it with numbers and noise.

translation: Does every person among them aspire to enter a garden of pleasure
commentary:
**أَيَطْمَعُ كُلُّ ٱمْرِئٍ مِّنْهُمْ أَن يُدْخَلَ جَنَّةَ نَعِيمٍ**

"Does every person among them aspire to be admitted into a garden of pleasure?"

Here Allah ﷻ exposes what lies beneath their aggressive defensiveness. أَيَطْمَعُ indicates real aspiration and hope, not mere wishful thinking. When they hear about who will enter Paradise, their objection—"Are you saying we won't enter Paradise?"—actually reveals that they hope to enter it themselves. Despite their disbelief, despite their opposition, despite their mockery, somewhere inside they harbor the expectation that they too will be among the blessed.

The Prophet ﷺ doesn't need to answer their rhetorical challenges. Allah ﷻ answers for him.

translation: No! Indeed, We have created them from that which they know
commentary:
**كَلَّآ ۖ إِنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰهُم مِّمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ**

"No! Indeed, We created them from that which they know."

كَلَّآ —absolutely not! Then comes the reminder: إِنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰهُم مِّمَّا يَعْلَمُونَ—"We created them from that which they know." What they know well is the despised fluid, the sperm that people wash off their clothes when it stains them. Allah ﷻ reminds them of their origins, their humble beginnings, the reality of their creation from something they themselves consider impure and insignificant. 

This is not merely about biological origin—it's about recognizing your place. You come rushing in with arrogance, making demands, questioning the Messenger ﷺ, but where did you come from? What are you made of? Know your reality before you presume to challenge your Creator's message.

translation: So I swear by the Lord of [all] risings and settings that indeed We are able
commentary:
**فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِرَبِّ ٱلْمَشَـٰرِقِ وَٱلْمَغَـٰرِبِ إِنَّا لَقَـٰدِرُونَ**

"So I swear by the Lord of the east and the west that indeed We are able."

After reminding them of their insignificance, Allah ﷻ asserts His magnificence. He swears by Himself as رَبِّ ٱلْمَشَـٰرِقِ وَٱلْمَغَـٰرِبِ —the Master of all the easts and all the wests. Not just the east and west, but every point where the sun rises and sets throughout the year, every horizon, every boundary of the earth. This encompasses all of creation in its vastness.

إِنَّا لَقَـٰدِرُونَ—"Indeed We are completely capable." Capable of what? The next ayah specifies.

translation: To replace them with better than them; and We are not to be outdone
commentary:
**عَلَىٰٓ أَن نُّبَدِّلَ خَيْرًا مِّنْهُمْ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ**

"To replace them with better than them; and We are not to be outdone."

Allah ﷻ tells His Messenger ﷺ: I don't need them. نُّبَدِّلَ خَيْرًا مِّنْهُمْ—I can replace all of them with people far better than they are. You don't need their validation, their acceptance, their approval. وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ—and no one can outpace Us, no one will escape Our reach. When We decide to replace them, it will happen. When We decide to punish them, no one will evade it.

This is profound comfort for the Messenger ﷺ and every believer who faces mockery and opposition. The message doesn't depend on its opponents accepting it. Allah ﷻ is fully capable of bringing forth a people who will cherish and uphold the truth.

translation: So leave them to converse vainly and amuse themselves until they meet their Day which they are promised
commentary:
**فَذَرْهُمْ يَخُوضُوا۟ وَيَلْعَبُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يُلَـٰقُوا۟ يَوْمَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى يُوعَدُونَ**

"So leave them to converse vainly and amuse themselves until they meet their Day which they are promised."

فَذَرْهُمْ—leave them, let them be. This is divine instruction to not be consumed by their opposition. يَخُوضُوا۟—let them wade into their discussions, their arguments, their debates. The verb خَاضَ means to wade through water or to plunge into conversation, often with the connotation of speaking about things carelessly or engaging in vain talk. Let them run their mouths. Let them talk their trash.

وَيَلْعَبُوا۟—and let them play, let them amuse themselves with their mockery and their games. You don't need to respond to every criticism, every attack, every challenge. Some people are committed to drowning in doubt, committed to pulling others into uncertainty. They call it being "open-minded," but true conviction isn't about perpetually doubting what you know to be true. If someone asks, "Are you willing to consider that your religion might be wrong?" after you've reached certainty, that's not open-mindedness they're requesting—it's that you join them in their doubt.

حَتَّىٰ يُلَـٰقُوا۟ يَوْمَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى يُوعَدُونَ—until they meet their Day which has been promised to them. Let them continue until reality catches up with them, until the Day they've been warned about arrives and they can no longer deny.

translation: The Day they will emerge from the graves rapidly as if they were, toward an erected idol, hastening
commentary:
**يَوْمَ يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ ٱلْأَجْدَاثِ سِرَاعًا كَأَنَّهُمْ إِلَىٰ نُصُبٍ يُوفِضُونَ**

"The Day they will emerge from the graves rapidly, as if they were racing toward an erected idol."

يَوْمَ يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ ٱلْأَجْدَاثِ—the Day they will come out from the ajdath. The word جَدَث refers to an unmarked grave, a burial place that leaves no trace. When you look at an open field, you don't know how many people might be buried there from centuries past. Someone who drowned in the ocean, their remains at the bottom where no one found them—that's a jadath. Someone who died in the desert and was covered by sand, someone who fell from a cliff and was consumed by animals and earth—these are the ajdath, the forgotten graves.

On that Day, everyone will emerge سِرَاعًا —rapidly, quickly, without delay. كَأَنَّهُمْ إِلَىٰ نُصُبٍ يُوفِضُونَ—as if they are racing toward a nusub. The word نُصُب referred to a large stone used as a finish line in races, or alternatively, it can refer to an idol that was erected and worshipped. Both meanings are profound here.

They're racing toward their destination like athletes sprint toward a finish line, except they have no control over this race. Or like prey running toward the very predator that will destroy them, racing into their own trap. The verb يُوفِضُونَ captures this—rushing forward, unable to stop themselves, compelled toward their fate.

The image is striking: those who used to rush toward the Prophet ﷺ to intimidate and interrupt will rush again, but this time toward Allah's judgment, without any ability to resist or delay.

translation: Their eyes humbled, humiliation will cover them. That is the Day which they had been promised
commentary:
**خَـٰشِعَةً أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْيَوْمُ ٱلَّذِى كَانُوا۟ يُوعَدُونَ**

"Their eyes humbled, humiliation will cover them. That is the Day which they had been promised."

خَـٰشِعَةً أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ—their eyes will be khashi'ah, filled with fear, lowered in humility and terror. The gaze that once looked at the Prophet ﷺ with mockery and contempt will be transformed into one of absolute submission and dread.

تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ—humiliation will cover them, will pile upon them layer after layer. The word تَرْهَقُ means to overcome, to pile on top of. The ذِلَّةٌ—the humiliation, the disgrace—will be comprehensive and inescapable, covering them completely.

ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْيَوْمُ ٱلَّذِى كَانُوا۟ يُوعَدُونَ—that is the Day which they had been promised. This refrain echoes throughout these final ayahs. Allah ﷻ keeps emphasizing: the Day that's promised, the Day you were warned about. Why? Because the central concept of this entire surah concerns questions and promises. People ask questions, but when the answers come, they don't change their behavior accordingly. They break their promises while Allah ﷻ certainly will keep His.

The surah that opened with someone mockingly asking about the punishment concludes with that punishment described—not as an abstract theological concept, but as a definite appointment that will be kept. Allah ﷻ does not break His promises, and this is both a comfort to the believers and a warning to those who reject. The Day will come. The promise will be fulfilled. The question is not whether, but only how we will meet it—as those who guarded their prayers and their character, or as those who played and waded in vain talk until reality overtook them.
